title: Identify marginal and conditional distributions
description: Practice determining if a distribution from a two-way table is a marginal or conditional distribution.

assessment_items.question_content:
James is interested in the relationship between weather conditions and train delays. For a year, James records the conditions each day as well as whether this train arrives on time or is delayed. Here are his results:

Weather condition|On-time|Delayed | Total
- | - | - | -
Sunny| $167$  | $3$ | $170$
Cloudy| $115$ | $5$ | $120$
Rainy| $40$ | $15$ | $55$
Snowy| $8$ | $12$ | $20$
Total| $330$ | $35$ | $365$

**The graph of which distribution is shown below?**

![A segmented bar graph plots relative frequency versus arrival status. There is a single bar representing total. It is divided into four sections representing weather conditions. From bottom to top, the sections represent snowy, rainy, cloudy, and sunny. The percentages for each are as follows. Snowy, 5. Rainy, 15. Cloudy, 35. Sunny, 45. All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d51e06fada3d6f77438202eae9898dac2c21a1a3)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Marginal vs. conditional distribution

The given distribution describes the *totals* from the weather conditions.

Since we are looking at the *totals* for each weather condition, this is considered a *marginal distribution.*

##Variable

As we can see from the legend, this is a distribution of *weather conditions* (sunny, cloudy, rainy, snowy), not of arrival status.

##Answer

The graph shows the marginal distribution of weather conditions.

choices.content: The marginal distribution of arrival status
The marginal distribution of weather conditions
The conditional distribution of arrival status for sunny days
The conditional distribution of weather conditions for on-time trains

assessment_items.question_content:
James is interested in the relationship between weather conditions and train delays. For a year, James records the conditions each day as well as whether this train arrives on time or is delayed. Here are his results:

Weather condition|On-time|Delayed | Total
- | - | - | -
Sunny| $167$  | $3$ | $170$
Cloudy| $115$ | $5$ | $120$
Rainy| $40$ | $15$ | $55$
Snowy| $8$ | $12$ | $20$
Total| $330$ | $35$ | $365$

**The graph of which distribution is shown below?**

![A segmented bar graph plots relative frequency versus weather condition. There are four bars representing sunny, cloudy, rainy, and snowy, respectively. Each bar extends from 0 to 100 percent and is divided into two parts. The lower represents, delayed, and the upper represents, on-time. Sunny is divided at 1 percent. Cloudy is divided at 3 percent. Rainy is divided at 27 percent. Snowy is divided at 60 percent. All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ffbbb8712bc814df94d8ea8c2caa9e7b93d9430c)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Variable 

Each bar of the graph shows a distribution, as percentages, of *arrival status* (on-time, delayed).

##Marginal vs. conditional distribution

Each bar is restricted to a certain weather condition.

Since we are looking at the distributions of arrival status separately for each weather condition, rather than looking only at totals, these are considered *conditional distributions.*

##Answer

This graph shows the conditional distributions of arrival status for each weather condition.

choices.content: The conditional distributions of weather condition for each on-time status
The conditional distributions of arrival status for each weather condition
The marginal distribution of weather condition
The marginal distribution of arrival status

assessment_items.question_content:
Each day of Spirit Week at Alek's school, students have the option of dressing up according to a different theme. On Tuesday of Spirit Week, Alek recorded the number of students at his school by class and style of dress. Here is his data:

Class | Dressed for Spirit Week | Dressed normally | Total
-|-|-
Freshman | $175$ | $73$ | $248$
Sophomore | $160$ | $38$ | $198$
Junior | $184$ | $47$ | $231$
Senior | $177$ | $3$ | $180$
Total |  $696$ | $161$ | $857$

**The graph of which distribution is shown below?**
![A segmented bar graph plots relative frequency versus style of dress. There is a single bar representing total. It is divided into two sections, with the bottom representing dressed normally and the top representing dressed for spirit week. The bar is divided at approximately 19%.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/33911a21a0e7d690b273019367c9e2bba533a5c6)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Marginal vs. conditional distribution

The given distribution describes the *totals* from the style of dress.

Since we are looking at the *totals* for each style of dress, this is considered a *marginal distribution.*

##Variable

As we can see from the legend, this is a distribution of *style of dress* (dressed for Spirit Week, dressed normally), not of class.

##Answer

The graph shows the marginal distribution of style of dress.

choices.content: The marginal distribution of class
The marginal distribution of style of dress
The conditional distribution of class for students dressed for Spirit Week
The conditional distribution of style of dress for juniors

assessment_items.question_content:
Each day of Spirit Week at Alek's school, students have the option of dressing up according to a different theme. On Tuesday of Spirit Week, Alek recorded the number of students at his school by class and style of dress. Here is his data:

Class | Dressed for Spirit Week | Dressed normally | Total
-|-|-
Freshman | $175$ | $73$ | $248$
Sophomore | $160$ | $38$ | $198$
Junior | $184$ | $47$ | $231$
Senior | $177$ | $3$ | $180$
Total |  $696$ | $161$ | $857$

**The graph of which distribution is shown below?**

![A segmented bar graph plots relative frequency versus type of dress. There is a single bar representing dressed for spirit week that extends from 0 to 100 percent. It is divided into four parts representing class. From bottom to top, the sections represent senior, junior, sophomore, and freshman. The percentages for each section are as follows. Senior, 24. Junior, 32. Sophomore, 21. Freshman, 23. All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/88b282e634b019e5643956d21731842e8738fb93)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Marginal vs. conditional distribution

The bar shows the percentage of students in each class, given that the student is dressed for Spirit Week.

Since we are looking at the percentages for each class only for students dressed for Spirit Week, and not from all students total, this is considered a *conditional distribution.*

##Answer

This graph shows the conditional distribution of class for students dressed for Spirit Week.

##Variable

As we can see from the legend, this is a distribution of *class* (freshman, sophomore, junior, senior), not of style of dress.

choices.content: The conditional distribution of class for students dressed for Spirit Week
The conditional distributions of style of dress for each class
The marginal distribution of class
The marginal distribution of style of dress

assessment_items.question_content:
Ajay has been recording his outcomes for three games that he has played this summer. Here is his data:

Game | Wins | Losses | Total
- | - | - | -
Chess | $5$ | $8$ | $13$
Checkers | $9$ | $2$ | $11$
Mancala | $3$ | $10$ | $13$
Total | $17$ | $20$ | $37$

**The graph of which distribution is shown below?**

![A segmented bar graph plots relative frequency versus game. There is a single bar representing total that extends from 0 to 100 percent. It is divided into three parts representing game types. From bottom to top, the sections represent mancala, checkers, and chess. The percentages for each section are as follows. Mancala, 38. Checkers, 24. Chess. 38. All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/f676b2ec88e779bb7404d044d43f33427899db3f)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Marginal vs. conditional distribution

The given distribution describes the *totals* from the games played.

Since we are looking at the *totals* for game played, this is considered a *marginal distribution.*

##Variable

As we can see from the legend, this is a distribution of *game played* (chess, checkers, mancala), not of outcome.

##Answer

The graph shows the marginal distribution of game played.

choices.content: The conditional distribution of outcome for each game
The conditional distribution of game for wins
The marginal distribution of outcome
The marginal distribution of game played

assessment_items.question_content:
Ajay has been recording his outcomes for three games that he has played this summer. Here is his data:

Game | Wins | Losses | Total
- | - | - | -
Chess | $5$ | $8$ | $13$
Checkers | $9$ | $2$ | $11$
Mancala | $3$ | $10$ | $13$
Total | $17$ | $20$ | $37$

**The graph of which distribution is shown below?**

![A segmented bar graph plots relative frequency versus game. There is a single bar representing mancala. It is divided into two sections with the bottom representing losses and the top representing wins. It is divided at approximately 78 percent.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/eed123538dcee36613a9076750429995326db9bb)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Variable

As we can see from the legend, this is a distribution of *outcome* (wins, losses), not of game played.

##Marginal vs. conditional distribution

The bar shows the percentage of each outcome, given that the game played is mancala.

Since we are looking at the percentages for each outcome only for games played of mancala, and not as a total from all games, this is considered a *conditional distribution.*

##Answer

This graph shows the conditional distribution of outcome for games played of mancala.

choices.content: The conditional distribution of outcome for games played of mancala
The conditional distributions of game played for losses
The marginal distribution of outcome
The marginal distribution of game played

assessment_items.question_content:
A veterinarian collected data about the kinds and sources of pets from his clients. Here is some of the data.

Pet source | Dogs | Cats | Total
- | - | - | -
Store or breeder | $65$ | $5$ | $70$
Stray | $11$ | $30$ | $41$
Shelter | $65$ | $49$ | $114$
Family or friends | $35$ | $31$ | $66$
Total | $175$ | $115$ | $290$

[//]: (Based on http://www.humanesociety.org/issues/pet_overpopulation/facts/pet_ownership_statistics.html)

**The graph of which distribution is shown below?**

![A segmented bar graph plots relative frequency versus pet source. There is a single bar representing total that extends from 0 to 100 percent. It is divided into four parts representing sources. From bottom to top, the sections represent stray, store or breeder, family or friends, and shelter. The percentages for each section are as follows. Stray, 17. Store or breeder, 22. Family or friends, 21. Shelter, 40. All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/2c7d00d501e99f84a327579cdeffb30140d41132)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Marginal vs. conditional distribution

The given distribution describes the *totals* from the pet sources.

Since we are looking at the *totals* for pet sources, this is considered a *marginal distribution.*

##Variable

As we can see from the legend, this is a distribution of *pet source* (store or breeder, stray, shelter, family or friends), not of kinds of pets.

##Answer

The graph shows the marginal distribution of pet source.

choices.content: The conditional distribution of pet source for dogs
The conditional distribution of kind of pet for each pet source
The marginal distribution of pet source
This is the marginal distribution of kind of pet (dog or cat).

assessment_items.question_content:
A veterinarian collected data about the kinds and sources of pets from his clients. Here is some of the data.

Pet source | Dogs | Cats | Total
- | - | - | -
Store or breeder | $65$ | $5$ | $70$
Stray | $11$ | $30$ | $41$
Shelter | $65$ | $49$ | $114$
Family or friends | $35$ | $31$ | $66$
Total | $175$ | $115$ | $290$

[//]: (Based on http://www.humanesociety.org/issues/pet_overpopulation/facts/pet_ownership_statistics.html)

**The graph of which distribution is shown below?**

![A segmented bar graph plots relative frequency versus source of pet. There are four bars representing store or breeder, stray, shelter, and family or friends. Each is divided into two parts, with the bottom representing cats and the top representing dogs. Store or breeder is divided at 5. Stray is divided at 73. Shelter is divided at 42. Family or friends is divided at 45. All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/f5bfe66c56f2435318977bb412811c74a8f0391b)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Marginal vs. conditional distribution

Each bar is restricted to a certain pet source.

Since we are looking at the distributions separately for each source, rather than looking only at totals, these are considered *conditional distributions.*

##Variable

Each bar of the graph shows a distribution, as percentages, of *kind of pet* (cats, dogs), not of pet source.

##Answer

This graph shows the conditional distributions of kind of pet for each source.

choices.content: The conditional distributions of pet source for each kind of pet
The conditional distributions of kind of pet for each source
The marginal distribution of pet source
The marginal distribution of kind of pet (dog or cat).

assessment_items.question_content:
James is interested in the relationship between weather conditions and train delays. For a year, James records the conditions each day as well as whether this train arrives on time or is delayed. Here are his results:

Weather condition|On-time|$\boxed{\text{Delayed}}$ | Total
- | - | - | -
Sunny| $167$  | $\boxed{3}$ | $170$
Cloudy| $115$ | $\boxed{5}$ | $120$
Rainy| $40$ | $\boxed{15}$ | $55$
Snowy| $8$ | $\boxed{12}$ | $20$
Total| $330$ | $\boxed{35}$ | $365$

**What is the best description of the distribution boxed in the third column of the table above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Variable

The boxed column shows frequencies of different weather types (sunny, cloudy, rainy, snowy), not of different arrival statuses.

##Marginal vs. conditional distribution

The given distribution describes the frequency of each weather condition, given that the train is delayed.

Since we are looking at the frequencies for each weather condition only for delayed trains, and not all trains (shown in the total column), this is considered a *conditional distribution.*

##Answer

This is the conditional distribution of weather type for delayed trains.

choices.content: This is the marginal distribution of arrival status.
This is the marginal distribution of weather type.
This is the conditional distribution of weather type for delayed trains.
This is the conditional distribution of arrival status for rainy weather.

assessment_items.question_content:
James is interested in the relationship between weather conditions and train delays. For a year, James records the conditions each day as well as whether this train arrives on time or is delayed. Here are his results:

Weather condition|On-time|Delayed | Total
- | - | - | -
Sunny| $167$  | $3$ | $170$
Cloudy| $115$ | $5$ | $120$
Rainy| $40$ | $15$ | $55$
Snowy| $8$ | $12$ | $20$
$\boxed{\text{Total}}$| $\boxed{330}$ | $\boxed{35}$ | $\boxed{365}$

**What is the best description of the distribution boxed in the last row of the table above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Variable

The boxed row shows frequencies of different arrival statuses (on time, delayed), not of different weather conditions.

##Marginal vs. conditional distribution

The given distribution describes the *totals* from the arrival statues.

Since we are looking at the *totals* for arrival statuses, this is considered a *marginal distribution.*

##Answer

This is the marginal distribution of arrival status.

choices.content: This is the marginal distribution of arrival status.
This is the marginal distribution of weather type.
This is the conditional distribution of weather type for on-time trains.
This is the conditional distribution of arrival status for snowy weather.

assessment_items.question_content:
Each day of Spirit Week at Alek's school, students have the option of dressing up according to a different theme. On Tuesday of Spirit Week, Alek recorded the number of students at his school by class and style of dress. Here is his data:

Class| Dressed for Spirit Week | Dressed normally | Total
-|-|-
$\boxed{\text{Freshman}}$ | $\boxed{175}$ | $\boxed{73}$ | $\boxed{248}$
Sophomore | $160$ | $38$ | $198$
Junior | $184$ | $47$ | $231$
Senior | $177$ | $3$ | $180$
Total |  $696$ | $161$ | $857$

**What is the best description of the distribution boxed in the second row of the table above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Variable

The boxed row shows frequencies of different styles of dress (dressed for Spirit Week, dressed normally), not of different classes.

##Marginal vs. conditional distribution

The given distribution describes the frequency of each style of dress, given that the student is a freshman.

Since we are looking at the frequencies for each style of dress only for freshmen, and not all students (shown in the total column), this is considered a *conditional distribution.*

##Answer

This is the conditional distribution of style of dress for freshmen.

choices.content: This is the marginal distribution of class.
This is the marginal distribution of style of dress.
This is the conditional distribution of class for students dressed normally.
This is the conditional distribution of style of dress for freshmen.

assessment_items.question_content:
Each day of Spirit Week at Alek's school, students have the option of dressing up according to a different theme. On Tuesday of Spirit Week, Alek recorded the number of students at his school by class and style of dress. Here is his data:

Class | Dressed for Spirit Week | Dressed normally | $\boxed{\text{Total}}$
-|-|-
Freshman | $175$ | $73$ | $\boxed{248}$
Sophomore | $160$ | $38$ | $\boxed{198}$
Junior | $184$ | $47$ | $\boxed{231}$
Senior | $177$ | $3$ | $\boxed{180}$
Total |  $696$ | $161$ | $\boxed{857}$

**What is the best description of the distribution boxed in the last column of the table above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Variable

The boxed column shows frequencies of different classes (freshman, sophomore, junior, senior), not of different styles of dress.

##Marginal vs. conditional distribution

The given distribution describes the *totals* from the classes.

Since we are looking at the *totals* for classes, this is considered a *marginal distribution.*

##Answer

This is the marginal distribution of class.

choices.content: This is the marginal distribution of style of dress.
This is the marginal distribution of class.
This is the conditional distribution of style of dress for each class.
This is the conditional distribution of class for  each style of dress.

assessment_items.question_content:
Ajay has been recording his outcomes for three games that he has played this summer. Here is his data:

Game | Wins | Losses | Total
- | - | - | -
Chess | $5$ | $8$ | $13$
$\boxed{\text{Checkers}}$ | $\boxed{9}$ | $\boxed{2}$ | $\boxed{11}$
Mancala | $3$ | $10$ | $13$
Total | $17$ | $20$ | $37$

**What is the best description of the distribution boxed in the second row of the table above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Variable

The boxed row shows frequencies of different outcomes (wins, losses), not of different games.

##Marginal vs. conditional distribution

The given distribution describes the frequency of each outcome, given that the game is checkers.

Since we are looking at the frequencies for each outcome only for checkers, and not all games (shown in the total column), this is considered a *conditional distribution.*

##Answer

This is the conditional distribution of outcome for checkers.

choices.content: This is the marginal distribution of game.
This is the marginal distribution of style of outcome.
This is the conditional distribution of game for wins.
This is the conditional distribution of outcome for checkers.

assessment_items.question_content:
Ajay has been recording his outcomes for three games that he has played this summer. Here is his data:

Game | Wins | Losses | Total
- | - | - | -
Chess | $5$ | $8$ | $13$
Checkers | $9$ | $2$ | $11$
Mancala | $3$ | $10$ | $13$
$\boxed{\text{Total}}$ | $\boxed{17}$ | $\boxed{20}$ | $\boxed{37}$

**What is the best description of the distribution boxed in the last row of the table above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Variable

The boxed row shows frequencies of different outcomes (wins, losses), not of different kinds of games.

##Marginal vs. conditional distribution

The given distribution describes the *totals* from the outcomes.

Since we are looking at the *totals* for outcomes, this is considered a *marginal distribution.*

##Answer

This is the marginal distribution of outcome.

choices.content: This is the marginal distribution of kind of game.
This is the marginal distribution of outcome.
This is the conditional distribution of game for each outcome.
This is the conditional distribution of outcome for each kind of game.

assessment_items.question_content:
A veterinarian collected data about the kinds and sources of pets from his clients. Here is some of the data.

Pet source | Dogs | $\boxed{\text{Cats}}$ | Total
- | - | - | -
Store or breeder | $65$ | $\boxed{5}$ | $70$
Stray | $11$ | $\boxed{30}$ | $41$
Shelter | $65$ | $\boxed{49}$ | $114$
Family or friends | $35$ | $\boxed{31}$ | $66$
Total | $175$ | $\boxed{115}$ | $290$

[//]: (Based on http://www.humanesociety.org/issues/pet_overpopulation/facts/pet_ownership_statistics.html)

**What is the best description of the distribution boxed in the third column of the table above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Variable

The boxed column shows frequencies of different pet sources (store or breeder, stray, shelter, family or friends), not of different kinds of pets.

##Marginal vs. conditional distribution

The given distribution describes the frequency of each source, given that the pet is a cat.

Since we are looking at the frequencies for each source only for cats, and not all pets (shown in the total column), this is considered a *conditional distribution.*

##Answer

This is the conditional distribution of pet source for cats.

choices.content: This is the marginal distribution of pet source.
This is the marginal distribution of kind of pet (dog or cat).
This is the conditional distribution of pet source for cats.
This is the conditional distribution of kind of pet for pets from family and friends.

assessment_items.question_content:
A veterinarian collected data about the kinds and sources of pets from his clients. Here is some of the data.

Pet source | Dogs | Cats | $\boxed{\text{Total}}$
- | - | - | -
Store or breeder | $65$ | $5$ | $\boxed{70}$
Stray | $11$ | $30$ | $\boxed{41}$
Shelter | $65$ | $49$ | $\boxed{114}$
Family or friends | $35$ | $31$ | $\boxed{66}$
Total | $175$ | $115$ | $\boxed{290}$

**What is the best description of the distribution boxed in the last column of the table above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Variable

The boxed column shows frequencies of different pet sources (store or breeder, stray, shelter, family or friends), not of different kinds of pets.

##Marginal vs. conditional distribution

The given distribution describes the *totals* from the pet sources.

Since we are looking at the *totals* for pet sources, this is considered a *marginal distribution.*

##Answer

choices.content: This is the marginal distribution of pet source.
This is the marginal distribution of kind of pet (dog or cat).
This is the conditional distribution of pet source for dogs.
This is the conditional distribution of kind of pet for pets from a store or breeder.
